flashcards.back: The study of abstract computing machines and problems they can solve. It explores limits of computation and classifies problems based on inherent difficulty.
A finite automaton where for each state and input symbol, there is exactly one transition to a next state. Formally defined as M = (Q, Σ, δ, q0, F).
A finite automaton where for each state and input symbol, there can be zero, one, or multiple transitions to next states. Can be converted to equivalent DFA using subset construction.
A pattern notation used to describe regular languages. Uses operators: union (+ or |), concatenation, and Kleene star (*) to represent sets of strings.
A grammar G = (V, Σ, P, S) where V is non-terminals, Σ is terminals, P is production rules, and S is start symbol. Productions are of form A → α where α ∈ (V∪Σ)*.
A finite automaton equipped with a stack memory. Can recognize context-free languages. Has transition function δ: Q × (Σ∪{ε}) × (Γ∪{ε}) → P(Q × Γ*).
A theoretical computing device with infinite tape, read-write head, and finite control. Can simulate any algorithmic computation. Formal definition: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, B, F).
States that any computable function can be computed by a Turing machine. Serves as a definition of computability.
Class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time O(n^k). Includes problems like sorting, shortest path, primality testing.
Class of decision problems verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine. Solutions can be checked efficiently but finding them may be hard.
A problem is NP-complete if: 1) It is in NP, and 2) Every NP problem can be reduced to it in polynomial time. Examples: SAT, Clique, Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Path.
Problems at least as hard as NP-complete problems. Not necessarily in NP (may not have polynomial-time verifiable solutions). Optimization versions of NP-complete problems are NP-hard.
A problem for which there exists an algorithm that always halts and gives correct yes/no answer. Example: membership problem for regular languages.
A problem for which no algorithm exists that always halts and gives correct answer. Example: Halting Problem, Post Correspondence Problem.
The problem of determining whether an arbitrary program halts on given input. Proven undecidable by Alan Turing using diagonalization argument.
